mainTopic: (continues) מה לי הן מה לי דמיהן · זוזי דאינשי עבדי ליה ספסירותיה · כל אגר נטר ליה אסור
overview: (1) Rava extends the principle of 'מה לי הן מה לי דמיהן' to permit fixing prices at market rates even without produce, and Abaye challenges this with the case of 'סאה בסאה'. (2) The Gemara discusses the role of a 'ספסיר' (money changer/broker) and the permissibility of transactions involving them, and the concept of 'מי שפרע'. (3) Rav Nachman's general rule that any payment for waiting is forbidden, and the case of exchanging coins at a different rate. (4) The law of finding extra change in a loan, and the distinction between a mistake ('טעות הדעת') and a gift, including the concern of 'הבלעה בחשבון'. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.topic: (continues) מה לי הן מה לי דמיהן
sugyaBoundaries.gist: Rava extends the principle of 'מה לי הן מה לי דמיהן' to permit fixing prices at market rates even without produce, and Abaye challenges this with the case of 'סאה בסאה'.
The Gemara discusses the role of a 'ספסיר' (money changer/broker) and the permissibility of transactions involving them, and the concept of 'מי שפרע'.
sugyaBoundaries.openingFormula: אמר ליה אדא בר אבא לרבא

sugyaBoundaries.gist: Rav Nachman's general rule that any payment for waiting is forbidden, and the case of exchanging coins at a different rate.
The law of finding extra change in a loan, and the distinction between a mistake ('טעות הדעת') and a gift, including the concern of 'הבלעה בחשבון'. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: רַב פַּפָּא וְרַב הוּנָא: objection from the בְּרִיתָא
steps.stepSummary: רַב פָּפָּא and רַב הוּנָא challenge רָבָא's ruling with a baraita that prohibits fixing prices without possession.
steps.whatsHappening: A direct objection is raised against רָבָא's ruling. רַב פַּפָּא and רַב הוּנָא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרַב יְהוֹשֻׁעַ raise an objection.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The בְּרִיתָא explicitly states that fixing prices is permitted only if the seller has the produce, and prohibited if he doesn't. This directly contradicts רָבָא's extension of רַבִּי יַנַּאי's principle.

keyTerms.term: אִם יֵשׁ לוֹ – מוּתָר, אִם אֵין לוֹ – אָסוּר
keyTerms.meaning: if he has it - permitted, if he doesn't have it - prohibited

steps.whatToRemember: The בְּרִיתָא is the source of the prohibition.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a קוֹשְׁיָא, not a שְׁאֵלָה.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the need for a distinction between loan and sale.

phrases.aramaic: אֵיתִיבֵיהּ רַב פָּפָּא וְרַב הוּנָא בְּרֵיהּ
phrases.english: Rav Pappa and Rav Huna, son of Rav Yehoshua, raised an objection to Rava:
All of them,
If he has it – permitted,
If he does not have it – prohibited!

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לְהוּ: הָתָם הַלְוָאָה, הָכָא זְבִינֵי.
phrases.english: He said to them: There it is a loan, here it is sales.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֵיתִיבֵיהּ רַב פָּפָא וְרַב הוּנָא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרַב יְהוֹשֻׁעַ לְרָבָא: כּוּלָּם, אִם יֵשׁ לוֹ – מוּתָּר, אִם אֵין לוֹ – אָסוּר!

rashi.hebrew: אין לו אסור - בהא דרבי אושעיא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the baraita's prohibition of selling without possession applies specifically to the case of רבי אושעיא, distinguishing it from the current discussion.

steps.title: רַבָּה וְרַב יוֹסֵף: reason for permitting fixing prices without possession
steps.stepSummary: רַבָּה and רַב יוֹסֵף explain the rationale behind permitting fixing prices at market rates even without possession.
steps.whatsHappening: A new teaching is introduced to explain the underlying logic. רַבָּה and רַב יוֹסֵף argue that the buyer derives no benefit from the transaction.
steps.deeperAnalysis: They claim the buyer could have bought the produce himself at a lower price in nearby markets. Therefore, the seller's lack of possession doesn't create an interest concern, as the buyer gains no advantage.

keyTerms.meaning: your favor is taken and thrown on the thorns
what benefit have you given me?
I would buy it in Hini and Shili cheaply

steps.whatToRemember: The rationale is based on the buyer's lack of benefit.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a מִימְרָא, not a question or challenge.
steps.whyThisMatters: This provides the economic reasoning behind the halachic ruling.

phrases.english: Rabba and Rav Yosef both say:

phrases.aramaic: מַאי טַעְמָא אֲמַרוּ רַבָּנַן פּוֹסְקִין עַל
phrases.english: What is the reason that the Sages said one may set a price by the…

phrases.aramaic: שַׁעַר שֶׁבַּשּׁוּק וְאַף עַל פִּי שֶׁאֵין לוֹ?
phrases.english: and even though he does not have it?

phrases.aramaic: דְּאָמַר לֵיהּ: שְׁקִילָא טֵיבוּתָיךְ וְשַׁדְיָיא אַחִיזְרֵי.
phrases.english: Because he says to him: Take your favor and throw it on the thorns.
What benefit have you done for me?
If I had coins in my hand –
I would buy them in Hini and Shili at a low price.

steps.triggerLanguage: רַבָּה וְרַב יוֹסֵף דְּאָמְרִי תַּרְוַיְיהוּ: מַאי טַעְמָא אֲמַרוּ רַבָּנַן פּוֹסְקִין עַל שַׁעַר שֶׁבַּשּׁוּק וְאַף עַל פִּי שֶׁאֵין לוֹ? דְּאָמַר לֵיהּ: שְׁקִילָא טֵיבוּתָיךְ וְשַׁדְיָיא אַחִיזְרֵי. מַאי אַהְנֵית לִי? אִי הֲווֹ לִי זוּזֵי בִּידֵי – הֲוָה מְזַבֵּנְינָא בְּהִנֵי וּבְשִׁילֵי בְּזוּלָא.

rashi.hebrew: היני ושילי - מקומות הן שהיו סמוכין לפומבדיתא שהיו רבה ורב יוסף יושבין בה כלומר בכל מקום הוא מוצא סביבותיו בדמים הללו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that היני and שילי are specific locations near פומבדיתא where רבה and רב יוסף resided, illustrating that the buyer could easily find produce at similar prices in the surrounding area.

steps.title: אַבָּיֵי: challenge from the case of סְאָה בִּסְאָה
steps.stepSummary: אַבָּיֵי challenges רַב יוֹסֵף's rationale by applying it to the case of lending a se'a for a se'a.
steps.whatsHappening: A direct challenge is raised against the rationale provided by רַב יוֹסֵף. אַבָּיֵי argues that if the rationale is that the lender derives no benefit, then lending a סְאָה בִּסְאָה should also be permitted.
steps.deeperAnalysis: אַבָּיֵי claims the lender could have kept the wheat and sold it later at a higher price. This would imply that the loan doesn't benefit the lender, contradicting the prohibition of interest.

keyTerms.meaning: will the wheat burn in my warehouse?

steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is based on the principle of 'no benefit' applied to loans.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a קוֹשְׁיָא, not a שְׁאֵלָה.
steps.whyThisMatters: This tests the consistency of the rationale provided by רַב יוֹסֵף.

phrases.english: Abaye said to Rav Yosef:

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא מֵעַתָּה מוּתָּר לִלְוֹת סְאָה בִּסְאָה,
phrases.english: But from now, is it permitted to lend a se’a for a se’a,
Because he can say to him:
Your favor is taken and thrown on the thorns,
For he says to him: Will the wheat burn in my warehouse?

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ: הָתָם הַלְוָאָה, הָכָא – זְבִינֵי.
phrases.english: He said to him: There it is a loan, here – it is a sale.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ אַבָּיֵי לְרַב יוֹסֵף: אֶלָּא מֵעַתָּה מוּתָּר לִלְוֹת סְאָה בִּסְאָה, מִשּׁוּם דְּמָצֵי אֲמַר לֵיהּ: שְׁקִילָא טֵיבוּתָיךְ וְשַׁדְיָיא אַחִיזְרֵי, דְּאָמַר לֵיהּ: חִטֵּי דְּקָדְחִי בַּאֲכַלְבַּאי?

rashi.hebrew: חטי דקדחי באכלבאי - בתמיה וכי אם הנחתי חטים שלי באוצרי כלום היו גשמים יורדים עליהן שיהיו צומחות בהן צמחים להתקלקל לישנא אחרינא דקדחי לשון חמימות כמו (ישעיהו סד) כקדח אש:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Aramaic phrase חטי דקדחי באכלבאי is a rhetorical question implying that wheat stored in a granary does not sprout or spoil due to rain, and offers an alternative interpretation that קדחי refers to heat like fire.

steps.title: Challenge: The buyer benefits from the broker's fee
steps.stepSummary: Adda bar Abba challenges Rava's ruling by pointing out that the buyer benefits from the transaction because he saves the cost of hiring a broker.
steps.whatsHappening: Adda bar Abba challenges רַבָּה's ruling on advance payment by noting the buyer saves a broker's fee. This potential gain might invalidate the transaction.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The buyer avoids paying a סַפְסֵירָא, which constitutes a financial benefit. רַבָּה clarifies the buyer still pays the fee to the seller, neutralizing the gain. רַב אַשֵּׁי adds that ready cash acts as its own broker.
steps.whatToRemember: The discussion centers on whether the buyer's potential savings on a broker's fee constitutes a benefit that affects the transaction's validity.
steps.confusionAlert: Rav Ashi's statement that 'a person's dinars serve as a broker' means that having ready cash eliminates the need for a middleman, thus no extra cost is incurred.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes that the presence of a potential financial benefit must be neutralized for the transaction to be permissible under the discussed conditions.

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ אַדָּא בַּר אַבָּא לְרָבָא:
phrases.english: Adda bar Abba said to Rava:
And behold, he needs to give a dinar to a broker!

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ: דְּקָא יָהֵיב לֵיהּ נָמֵי.
phrases.english: He said to him: He also gives him the broker's fee.
Rav Ashi said:

phrases.aramaic: זוּזֵי דְּאִינָשֵׁי אִינְהוּ עָבְדִי לֵיהּ סַפְסֵירוּתֵיהּ.
phrases.english: A person's dinars, they make for him his brokerage.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ אַדָּא בַּר אַבָּא לְרָבָא: וְהָא בָּעֵי לְמִיתַּב זוּזֵי לְסַפְסֵירָא!

rashi.hebrew: והא בעי למיתב זוזי לספסירא - היכי אמרינן מאי אהני לפסוק על שער שבשוק והא אהני דבעי למיתב זוזי לספסירא סרסור שמבקש למוכרי חטין נמצא משתכר זוזי בהקדמת המעות:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that אַדָּא בַּר אַבָּא's challenge is that the buyer benefits from the transaction by avoiding the broker's fee, which should invalidate the ruling that the transaction is permissible.

rashi.hebrew: ומשני דיהיב - צריך הלוקח ליתן למוכר דמי הסרסור:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רָבָא's response is that the buyer must pay the broker's fee to the seller, thereby neutralizing the benefit.

rashi.hebrew: רב אשי אמר - לא צריך זוזי דאיניש עבדי ליה ספסירות החמרין הולכין אצל בעלי המעות להוליך לו תבואה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רַב אָשֵׁי's statement means that the buyer's own money acts as a broker, so no additional fee is required.

steps.title: Rabba and Rav Yosef: The buyer must present himself at the granary
steps.stepSummary: Rabba and Rav Yosef rule that a buyer who pays in advance must present himself at the seller's granary when the produce is being threshed.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces a new ruling from רַבָּה and רַב יוֹסֵף regarding advance payments. The buyer must physically present himself at the granary during threshing.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This ruling imposes a specific obligation on the buyer. The Gemara will now analyze the purpose and necessity of this physical presence.
steps.whatToRemember: The obligation is specifically to 'present himself' (לְאִיתְחֲזוֹיֵי) at the granary.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a new topic, not a direct continuation of the broker discussion.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the central question of the sugya: why is physical presence required?

phrases.english: Rabba and Rav Yosef both say:

phrases.aramaic: הַאי מַאן דְּיָהֵיב זוּזֵי אַתַּרְעָא חֲרִיפָא,
phrases.english: This one who gives coins at the sharp threshing floor,
needs to be seen at the house of threshing.

steps.triggerLanguage: רַבָּה וְרַב יוֹסֵף דְּאָמְרִי תַּרְוַיְיהוּ: הַאי מַאן דְּיָהֵיב זוּזֵי אַתַּרְעָא חֲרִיפָא, צָרִיךְ לְאִיתְחֲזוֹיֵי לְבֵי דָרֵי.

rashi.hebrew: האי מאן דיהיב זוזי אתרעא חריפא - הפוסק על הפירות עד שלא יצא השער הברור אלא שער הבכיר שהיא סמוך לקציר שהלקוטות מוכרין בזולא זה סאה וזה סאתים ותנן מתני' (דף עב:) דאם יש לו מותר לפסוק ואע"פ שלא יצא השער דקתני היה הוא תחילה לקוצרים פוסק עמו על הגדיש:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the term אתרעא חריפא refers to a preliminary price set before the market price is finalized, specifically near harvest time when prices fluctuate, and notes that the Mishnah permits such advance payment even before the final price is established.

rashi.hebrew: צריך הלוקח לאיתחזויי אבי דרי - של מוכר כשהוא דש וזורה תבואתו בגורן ולהכי נקיט תרעא חריפא שאין פוסקין עליו אלא למי שיש לו גורן דאילו שער הברור פוסקים אף למי שאין לו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the buyer must present himself at the seller's threshing floor during the threshing process, and explains that the specific mention of אתרעא חריפא implies this obligation applies only when the seller has a threshing floor, unlike the final market price which applies even without one.

steps.title: Challenge: What is the purpose of presenting himself?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the ruling by asking what purpose the buyer's presence serves, rejecting both acquisition and the curse of מי שפרע as reasons.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara questions the necessity of the buyer's physical presence. It considers and rejects two potential reasons: קניין or accepting the curse of מִי שֶׁפָּרַע.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Presence alone doesn't effect acquisition, and the curse applies even without presence. This creates a difficulty for the ruling, forcing a search for a different reason.
steps.whatToRemember: The two rejected reasons are קניין and מִי שֶׁפָּרַע.
steps.confusionAlert: The curse of מִי שֶׁפָּרַע is a standard Talmudic mechanism for binding agreements, but here it's deemed insufficient to require presence.
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces the Gemara to find a different reason for the obligation.

phrases.english: For what? If to acquire –
He does not acquire,
If to accept upon himself 'He Who exacted payment',

phrases.aramaic: כִּי לָא מִיתְחֲזֵי נָמֵי מְקַבֵּל עֲלֵיהּ ״מִי שֶׁפָּרַע״!
phrases.english: Even if he does not appear, he also accepts upon himself 'He Who exacted payment'!

steps.triggerLanguage: לְמַאי? אִי לְמִקְנֵא – הָא לָא קָנֵי, אִי לְקַבּוֹלֵי עֲלֵיהּ ״מִי שֶׁפָּרַע״, כִּי לָא מִיתְחֲזֵי נָמֵי מְקַבֵּל עֲלֵיהּ ״מִי שֶׁפָּרַע״!

rashi.hebrew: למאי - מיבעי האי דמתחזי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara is questioning the purpose of the buyer's physical presence (האי דמתחזי) in the transaction.

rashi.hebrew: אי למיקני - ממש שלא יוכל המוכר לחזור:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the proposed purpose of 'acquisition' (למיקני) refers to preventing the seller from retracting the sale.

rashi.hebrew: הא לא קני - בהכי אלא א"כ משך:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that physical presence alone does not effect acquisition (לא קני) unless the buyer performs a formal act of drawing the item (משך).

rashi.hebrew: אי לקבולי מוכר עליה מי שפרע - אם יחזיר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that accepting the curse of מי שפרע applies if the seller attempts to retract the sale.

steps.title: Resolution: The buyer's presence reassures the seller
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the difficulty by explaining that the buyer's presence reassures the seller that he hasn't found better produce elsewhere.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara explains the buyer must present himself to reassure the seller. This prevents the seller from suspecting the buyer found better produce elsewhere.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The buyer often pays multiple sellers in advance. Showing up confirms the deal is still on, providing סָמְכָא דַעְתֵּיהּ. This psychological reassurance prevents the seller from changing his mind.

keyTerms.meaning: he relies on him / his mind is reassured

steps.whatToRemember: The key concept is סָמְכָא דַעְתֵּיהּ — the seller's reliance on the buyer's commitment.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a psychological/commercial reason, not a formal halachic acquisition.
steps.whyThisMatters: This explains the practical necessity of the ruling.

phrases.english: Forever to accept upon himself "He Who exacted payment"
And whoever gives coins at the advance price
To two or three he gives
If he appears to him

phrases.aramaic: סָמְכָא דַּעְתֵּיהּ, וְאִי לָא – אָמַר לֵיהּ:
phrases.english: His mind is supported, and if not – he says to him:

phrases.aramaic: אָמֵינָא דְּאַשְׁכַּחְתְּ פֵּירֵי דְּשַׁפִּירֵי מִדִּידִי וּשְׁקַלְתְּ.
phrases.english: I said that you found better produce than mine and you took it.

steps.triggerLanguage: לְעוֹלָם לְקַבּוֹלֵי עֲלֵיהּ ״מִי שֶׁפָּרַע״, וּמַאן דְּיָהֵיב זוּזֵי אַתַּרְעָא [חֲרִיפָא], לְבֵי תְרֵי תְלָתָא יָהֵיב, אִי מִיתְחֲזֵי לֵיהּ – סָמְכָא דַעְתֵּיהּ, וְאִי לָא – אָמַר לֵיהּ: אָמֵינָא דְאַשְׁכַּחְתְּ פֵּירֵי דְשַׁפִּירֵי מִדִּידִי וּשְׁקַלְתְּ.

rashi.hebrew: סמכא דעתא - דכל חד וחד וידע דעליה קא סמיך האי לוקח וסחורה מקויימת היא ואין דעתו לחזור הלכך אם חזר בו מוכר איכא למילטייה במי שפרע:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the buyer's presence reassures the seller that the deal is firm and the buyer is relying on him, so if the seller tries to back out, he can be compelled to fulfill the sale through the mechanism of מי שפרע.

steps.title: Rav Ashi: Reassurance can be given in the marketplace too
steps.stepSummary: Rav Ashi extends the logic: if the goal is reassurance, the buyer can provide it by finding the seller in the marketplace and confirming the deal.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב אַשֵּׁי applies the previous reasoning to a different scenario. He argues physical presence at the granary isn't strictly necessary.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Since the purpose is merely סָמְכָא דַעְתָּא, the buyer can achieve the same result by encountering the seller in the marketplace. This broadens the application of the principle.
steps.whatToRemember: The core requirement is the seller's reassurance, not the specific location.
steps.confusionAlert: This doesn't negate the previous ruling but shows its underlying principle can be fulfilled differently.
steps.whyThisMatters: It highlights that the halacha is driven by the practical need for reassurance rather than a formal ritual.

phrases.english: Rav Ashi said:
Now that you have said due to reliance of mind,

phrases.aramaic: אֲפִילּוּ אַשְׁכְּחֵיהּ בְּשׁוּקָא וַאֲמַר לֵיהּ – סָמְכָא דַּעְתֵּיהּ.
phrases.english: Even if he finds him in the marketplace and says to him – his mind relies.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַב אָשֵׁי: הַשְׁתָּא דְאָמַרְתְּ מִשּׁוּם מִסְמָךְ דַעְתָּא, אַפִּילּוּ אַשְׁכַּחְיֵהּ בְּשׁוּקָא וַאֲמַר לֵיהּ – סָמְכָא דַעְתֵּיהּ.

rashi.hebrew: השתא דאמרת - דאי מיתחזי מיסמך דעתא הוא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the underlying principle of the previous step is that the buyer's intent to pay is established through the seller's reliance on the buyer's word (מִסְמָךְ דַּעְתָּא).

rashi.hebrew: אפילו - לא מיתחזי אבי דרי נמי אלא אשכחיה בשוקא לאחר זמן ואמר ליה חזי דעלך סמכינא איכא מי שפרע:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that even if the buyer does not appear at the granary, encountering the seller later in the marketplace and stating 'I relied on you' is sufficient to establish the seller's reliance (סָמְכָא דַּעְתֵּיהּ) and thus the buyer's obligation.

steps.title: רַב נַחְמָן's general principle: any payment for waiting is forbidden
steps.stepSummary: רַב נַחְמָן states a sweeping principle: any extra payment given in exchange for delaying collection is forbidden as רִבִּית.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with רַב נַחְמָן's foundational ruling on interest. He formulates a general rule regarding extra payments.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב נַחְמָן states כּל אגר נטר ליה אסור. If a lender receives extra money specifically for waiting, it is forbidden as רִבִּית. This sets the stage for the specific case that follows.
steps.whatToRemember: The principle is broad: any extra payment for waiting is forbidden.
steps.confusionAlert: The principle is broad: any extra payment for waiting is forbidden.
steps.whyThisMatters: This principle is the foundation for understanding why certain advance sales are forbidden.

phrases.english: Rav Naḥman said: The general rule of interest,
Any payment for his waiting –
is forbidden. And Rav Naḥman said:

phrases.aramaic: הַאי מַאן דְּיָהֵיב זוּזֵי לְקִירָאָה וְקָא
phrases.english: This one who gives coins to a wax-seller and
goes four four, and says to him:
I will give you five five,
they are with him – permitted,
they are not with him – forbidden.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר רַב נַחְמָן: כּלל דרִבִּית, כּל אגר נטר ליה אסור.

rashi.hebrew: אגר נטר ליה - שכר המתנה אי מוזיל גבי משום המתנה שמקדים לו המעות והמקח אין לו למוכר עכשיו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that אגר נטר ליה refers to a discount given for advancing payment, which is forbidden because the seller does not currently possess the goods to sell.

rashi.hebrew: איתנהו - אם בידו אבל אינם בעיר או אבד המפתח והוא דחוק למעות שרי דהוי ליה הלויני עד שיבא בני דתנן במתניתין (דף עה.) דמותר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that if the goods are physically in the seller's possession but inaccessible (e.g., lost key), the transaction is permitted as it resembles a loan until the son returns, which is allowed.

tosafot.hebrew: ואמר ליה יהיבנא לך חמשה בזוזא - פי' לזמן פלוני איתנהו שרי ליתנהו אסור אבל ד' בזוזא שרי לפסוק כיון שכן השער דפוסקין על שער שבשוק וא"ת אמאי אסור לפסוק חמשה בזוזא מאי שנא מטרשא דרב נחמן גופיה אמר לקמן (בבא מציעא דף סה.) טרשא שרי למכור סחורה בהמתנה ביותר מדמיה ובלבד שלא יפרש [לו] אם מעכשיו בפחות וי"ל כיון דאזלי הכא ד' בזוזא הוי כאילו פירש אם מעכשיו בפחות ואם לזמן פלוני ביותר ולפי זה לא שרי ר"נ לקמן טרשא אלא בדבר שאין שומתו ידוע כגון פרה או טלית אבל פירות אם יצא השער אסור לעשות מהן טרשא:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות distinguishes between setting a price based on market value (permitted) and setting a higher price for future delivery when the market price is known (forbidden), resolving the apparent contradiction with רב נחמן's ruling on טרשא by limiting טרשא to items without a fixed market value.

steps.title: Why teach this case? Isn't it obvious?
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא challenges the necessity of רַב נַחְמָן's teaching, arguing that the prohibition in the case where the wax is not present is obvious.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא attacks the redundancy of רַב נַחְמָן's specific teaching. It questions why the case needs to be taught at all.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא argues פשיטא! If the seller lacks the goods, the buyer is essentially lending money. This is clearly interest, so the teaching seems unnecessary.
steps.whatToRemember: The challenge is that the case seems to follow directly from the general principle.
steps.confusionAlert: The challenge is that the case seems to follow directly from the general principle.
steps.whyThisMatters: This challenge forces the גמרא to find a non-obvious case where the principle applies.

phrases.aramaic: פְּשִׁיטָא! לָא צְרִיכָא דְּאִית לֵיהּ אַשְׁרַאי בְּמָתָא.
phrases.english: It is obvious! It is not necessary that he has credit in the town.

phrases.aramaic: מַהוּ דְּתֵימָא: כֵּיוָן דְּאִית לֵיהּ אַשְׁרַאי בְּמָתָא –
phrases.english: What is it that you say: Since he has credit in the town –

phrases.aramaic: כְּ״עַד שֶׁיָּבֹא בְּנִי״ אוֹ ״עַד שֶׁאֶמְצָא מַפְתֵּחַ״ דָּמֵי,
phrases.english: Like 'until my son comes' or 'until I find the key' it is similar,

phrases.aramaic: קָא מַשְׁמַע לַן כֵּיוָן דִּמְחַסְּרִי גּוּבְיָינָא –
phrases.english: It teaches us: Since they lack collection –
Like one who does not have them it is similar.

rashi.hebrew: דאית ליה אשראי במתא - המוכר הזה אף הוא נתן מעות ופסק עם אחרים על שעוה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the seller has אשראי במתא because he has already given money to others and agreed on a price for wax, meaning he has a credit arrangement in the market.

tosafot.hebrew: מהו דתימא כעד שיבא בני דמי קמ"ל - וא"ת והא רב נחמן פסק לקמן בשילהי פירקין (בבא מציעא דף עה.) כהלל דאסר עד שיבא בני וי"ל דודאי דרך הלואה אסור אפילו סאה בסאה אבל דרך מקח וממכר כי הכא שרי אפי' ה' בארבע דאי לאו הכי תקשה דאפילו רבנן לקמן לא שרו אלא סאה בסאה אבל סאה בסאתים לא אלא כדפרישית דדרך מקח וממכר שרי טפי חמש בארבע מדרך הלואה אפילו סאה בסאה דהכי שרי לכ"ע וא"ת דטפי ה"ל לאתויי הכא דפוסק על הגדיש דדמיא טפי דהוי מקח וממכר וי"ל דפוסק עמו על הגדיש לא שרינן אלא לפסוק עמו בשער לקוטות אבל לפסוק שוי שיתא בחמשא לא כיון דאכתי לית ליה שעדיין צריך תיקון ואי מוזיל גביה כולי האי מחזי כאגר נטר אבל כשיש לו מזומן בעין אלא שמחוסר ביאת בנו או הבאת מפתח מותר אפילו שוי חמשא בארבע וה"ק נעשה כעד שיבא בני ושרי הכא לכ"ע כדשרי התם לרבנן:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות distinguishes between a loan (הלואה) and a sale (מקח וממכר), explaining that while a loan with interest is forbidden even at a 1:1 ratio, a sale transaction permits a higher markup (like 5 for 4) because it is not considered interest, resolving the apparent contradiction with רב נחמן's ruling elsewhere.

steps.title: רַב נַחְמָן: טוּפְיָינָא בִּכְדֵי שֶׁהַדַּעַת טוֹעָה
steps.stepSummary: רַב נַחְמָן rules that extra coins found in a loan must be returned if the amount is within the range of a plausible counting error, but is considered a gift if the amount is too large to be a mistake.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with a foundational ruling from רַב נַחְמָן regarding extra money found in a loan. He establishes a binary distinction based on the surplus magnitude.
steps.deeperAnalysis: If the טוּפְיָינָא is small enough to be a plausible counting error, the borrower must return it. If it is too large, it is presumed to be a מַתָּנָה. This sets up the need to define the threshold of a 'plausible mistake'.

keyTerms.meaning: within the range that the mind can err

steps.whatToRemember: The core principle is that small surpluses are presumed errors (and must be returned), while large surpluses are presumed gifts.
steps.confusionAlert: The term טוּפְיָינָא refers to the extra amount found, not the total loan.
steps.whyThisMatters: This ruling introduces the concept of הַבְלָעָה בַּחֶשְׁבּוֹן (absorbing a debt into a larger transaction) as a mechanism for returning stolen money or fulfilling obligations discreetly.

phrases.english: And Rav Naḥman said:

phrases.aramaic: הַאי מַאן דְּאוֹזֵיף פְּשִׁיטֵי מֵחַבְרֵיהּ וְאַשְׁכַּח בֵּיהּ טוּפְיָינָא,
phrases.english: This one who borrowed coins from his friend and found in them an excess,
If it is within the measure that the mind errs –
He is obligated to return it to him,
And if not –
A gift in the world it is that he gave him.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְאָמַר רַב נַחְמָן: הַאי מַאן דְּאוֹזֵיף פְּשִׁיטֵי מֵחַבְרֵיהּ וְאַשְׁכַּח בֵּיהּ טוּפְיָינָא...

rashi.hebrew: ואשכח טופיינא - זה שקיבל המעות מצא בהן יותר ממה שאמר לו חבירו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that טופיינא refers to the borrower discovering more money than the lender originally stated was lent.

rashi.hebrew: אי בכדי שהדעת טועה - דאיכא למימר טעה המלוה בחשבון ולא ידע בהן חייב זה להחזירו לו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that בכדי שהדעת טועה means the surplus is small enough to be attributed to the lender's counting error, thus obligating the borrower to return it.

steps.title: הֵיכִי דָמֵי בִּכְדֵי שֶׁהַדַּעַת טוֹעָה?
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא asks for a concrete definition of what constitutes a plausible counting error.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא asks for a concrete example of the threshold בִּכְדֵי שֶׁהַדַּעַת טוֹעָה. This is a setup line seeking practical parameters for the ruling.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The abstract term בִּכְדֵי שֶׁהַדַּעַת טוֹעָה needs operationalization. The גמרא seeks a real-world scenario to define the limit of a plausible error. This clarifies when a surplus is a mistake versus a gift.

keyTerms.meaning: what are the circumstances / how does it look?
within the range that the mind can err

phrases.english: How is it like 'where the mind errs'?

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַב אַחָא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרַב יוֹסֵף:
phrases.english: Rav Aḥa, son of Rav Yosef, said:
